concepts_addressed: Setup Fee as Churn Reduction Mechanism

frameworks.name: Discount Plus One-Time Fee Structure
frameworks.definition: A two-part offer that charges a deeply discounted first term to lower acquisition friction, then adds one or more separately named one-time fees to fund commissions and reduce churn.

frameworks.name: Four-Step One-Time Fee Creation
frameworks.definition: A four-step process to create any made-up fee: (1) pick fee name, (2) pick fee price, (3) pick reason why, (4) start charging, discounting, or waiving it.

claims.text: Alex Hormozi argues that a Discount Plus One-Time Fee offer structure serves two simultaneous functions: it attracts new customers via a deeply discounted first term (reducing purchase friction) and liquidates acquisition costs and sales commissions through a separately charged one-time fee. The combination means customer acquisition can be monetized without raising the recurring price.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
conditions: recurring service or defined-end program
business using commission-based sales
claims.mechanism: The discounted price lowers the psychological barrier to entry; the separate fee, which can be named anything (setup fee, enrollment fee, onboarding fee), recaptures revenue and directly funds sales commissions without the customer perceiving the full price.
bounded_by: Not applicable where advertising laws prohibit split-price presentation
Requires legal compliance review for each jurisdiction

claims.text: Alex Hormozi asserts that a discount must be large enough (in his practice 50% or higher) to actually change the behavior of a prospect who otherwise would not buy; discounts below that threshold merely give away margin without expanding the buyer pool.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
conditions: discount is the primary acquisition mechanism
offer has a recurring or defined-end structure
claims.mechanism: A shallow discount (10-20%) does not alter the purchase decision of a fence-sitting prospect; only a discount sufficient to move a non-buyer into a buyer actually grows the pool. Without that threshold effect, the discount is pure margin sacrifice on buyers who were already committed.

anchors.value: no new buyers; margin given away to existing buyers

bounded_by: Threshold level is speaker-defined and may vary by industry and price point

claims.text: Alex Hormozi explains that a single discount dollar amount can be presented in three distinct ways to the same prospect: (1) as a percentage off (e.g., '20% off first month'), (2) as an absolute dollar reduction (e.g., '$200 off first month'), or (3) as a specific first-month price (e.g., '$800 first month'). Testing which framing converts best is independent of the actual economics.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
conditions: discount-led offer
recurring or defined-end service

numbers.axis: framing variants for a $1,000/month service at 20% off

claims.text: Alex Hormozi claims that the higher the one-time setup fee charged at enrollment, the lower the subsequent churn, because the upfront financial commitment creates a sunk-cost barrier to exit that mirrors the barrier to entry.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
claims.speaker_term: higher barrier to entry, so too becomes the higher barrier to exit
claims.topic: Setup Fee as Churn Reduction Mechanism
conditions: recurring service where the customer must take action to receive value (e.g., physical therapy, weight loss coaching)
setup fee is materially significant relative to monthly recurring price
claims.mechanism: Sunk-cost psychology: a customer who has paid a large upfront fee frames quitting as surrendering that investment. The same logic that made the fee a barrier to entry (high perceived cost) becomes a barrier to exit (high perceived loss).
bounded_by: Effect diminishes for pure convenience services where the customer has no required ongoing action

claims.text: Alex Hormozi describes a multi-million dollar online weight loss coaching business that charges $5,000 at enrollment and then only $267 per month, achieving a client lifespan exceeding two years in an industry where typical churn occurs within three to four months. This demonstrates a massive disparity between the one-time fee and the recurring fee as a deliberate churn-reduction lever.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
claims.topic: Setup Fee as Churn Reduction Mechanism
conditions: service where customer behavior (compliance, showing up, executing the program) is required for outcome delivery
industry with historically high churn (3-4 month average)
claims.mechanism: The large upfront payment psychologically anchors the client to the program; leaving before achieving the result means forfeiting the $5,000 sunk cost. If a lapsed client wishes to return, they must pay the enrollment fee again, reinforcing commitment for new and returning clients alike.

numbers.axis: client lifespan by enrollment fee structure in weight loss coaching

claims.text: Alex Hormozi's early mentor John, who operated a tanning salon chain, observed that members who paid a $100 sign-up fee for a $10/month membership churned at near-zero rates, while members who paid only $19 down with $19/month churned at a substantially higher rate. This supports the principle that sign-up fee magnitude inversely predicts churn.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
claims.topic: Setup Fee as Churn Reduction Mechanism
conditions: low-price recurring membership service (tanning salon)
comparable monthly recurring rate across cohorts
claims.mechanism: Higher enrollment payment creates a sunk-cost commitment effect; members who invested more at sign-up have a stronger psychological stake in continuing.

numbers.axis: churn rate by enrollment fee in tanning salon memberships

bounded_by: Single operator observation; no controlled study cited

claims.text: Alex Hormozi asserts that the one-time fee name and rationale are entirely fabricated by the seller, but should be clearly communicated to the customer as something the seller is doing for them. Even if the seller never actually charges the fee (waiving it for every prospect), the mere existence of the fee functions as a discount anchor, creating perceived value equivalent to waiving a real charge.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
conditions: seller has flexibility to waive or charge the fee per prospect
fee is clearly named and explained, even if fabricated
claims.mechanism: Waiving a fee that the customer was told they would be charged creates perceived savings and goodwill without changing the actual price. The customer experiences the waived fee as a gift equivalent to its stated dollar value.
bounded_by: Must comply with local advertising and disclosure laws
If the fee is never charged and the seller always waives it, it functions as a marketing frame, not actual variable pricing

claims.text: Alex Hormozi argues that businesses should never negotiate price downward in real time after naming a price (e.g., offering $900 after a prospect resists a $1,000 price), because this destroys credibility and signals that the price was arbitrary. A pre-stated discount ('today it's $500, tomorrow it won't be') preserves seller authority because the terms were set before the conversation, not in response to resistance.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
conditions: sales interaction where price objection arises
seller has discretion over whether and how to discount
claims.mechanism: Post-resistance price reduction signals to the buyer that the original price was inflated and that further negotiation is possible, triggering a spiral of distrust and anchoring buyer expectation at an arbitrary lower number. A pre-set discount communicates that the seller controls the terms and the discount has an objective basis.
bounded_by: Applies to direct sales situations; may differ in negotiated contract or enterprise B2B contexts

claims.text: Alex Hormozi contends that the Discount Plus One-Time Fee structure requires a discount that is deeply enough discounted to be applied to just one component of the full offer (e.g., 80% off one of five service elements) rather than a shallow discount across the whole service, so the remaining four components are still sold at full price.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
conditions: multi-component service offer
seller reluctant to discount the full service deeply
claims.mechanism: By separating one low-cost or introductory component and discounting that component heavily, the seller can present a compelling discount headline while preserving full-price revenue on the remaining components. This avoids margin compression on the full service.

anchors.value: 20% off all five components; low marginal conversion lift; full margin compressed
80% off one component; four at full price; compelling headline with preserved margin on core service

claims.text: Alex Hormozi states that the Discount Plus One-Time Fee structure works for both recurring services and defined-end programs (e.g., a 12-week physical therapy program), and the same discount-plus-fee math applies to both: deeply discount the first period and charge a one-time enrollment fee to offset the discount and fund acquisition costs.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
conditions: recurring service OR defined-end program of fixed duration

numbers.axis: Discount Plus Fee applied to a 12-week physical therapy program at $1,000/month

anchors.region: first month (88% off at $120) + $1,000 setup fee

claims.text: Alex Hormozi observes that a discounted front-end offer (rather than a free offer) produces fewer surprised or skeptical customers at checkout because prospects who were told they would pay something arrive expecting to take out their credit card.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
conditions: offer uses a discounted first term rather than a completely free trial
customer is transacting at checkout
claims.mechanism: A free offer disrupts expectation at the moment of payment; a discounted offer sets prior expectation that payment is required. This eliminates the friction spike that arises when a free-trial customer is unexpectedly asked for a credit card.

claims.text: Alex Hormozi argues that making a one-time fee clearly named and rationale-explained (even if the rationale is entirely fabricated) is not morally problematic as long as the fee disclosure is transparent, and the seller delivers on whatever the fee is described as funding.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
conditions: seller names the fee and provides a stated purpose
seller actually does what they say the fee funds
bounded_by: Requires legal advertising compliance per jurisdiction

examples.text: In spring 2015, Alex Hormozi was approached in the parking lot of his gym location by a man named Owen, who introduced himself as a personal training manager whose gym had closed. Owen pitched Hormozi on letting his team of trainers use the gym's dead space to sell personal training packages. When Hormozi resisted, Owen revealed his commission structure: he would offer new clients a discounted first month and then charge an enrollment fee, which his salespeople would keep as their commission. Hormozi declined the partnership over trust concerns about an unvetted team, but recognized the offer structure as novel and effective on its own merits. This was Hormozi's first exposure to what he later formalized as the Discount Plus One-Time Fee structure.

A tanning salon operator referenced by Alex Hormozi as an early mentor named John ran two enrollment pricing tiers simultaneously. Tier one: $19 down and $19 per month, producing high churn. Tier two: $100 sign-up fee and $10 per month, producing near-zero churn despite having a lower monthly rate. John told Hormozi that the $100 barrier effectively created a matching exit barrier, with the $19-down cohort churning at dramatically higher rates.
examples.topic: Setup Fee as Churn Reduction Mechanism

examples.text: Alex Hormozi referenced a friend who runs a multi-million dollar online weight loss coaching business. That business charges $5,000 at enrollment and $267 per month afterward. In an industry where typical client tenure is three to four months, this business achieves average client lifespans exceeding two years. Hormozi attributed this entirely to the large upfront investment making departure psychologically costly: the client thinks 'I just paid $5,000, why would I ever quit?' If a client does leave and later wants to return, they must pay the $5,000 enrollment fee again.
examples.topic: Setup Fee as Churn Reduction Mechanism

practices.text: For any recurring service, test multiple framings of the same discount — percentage off, absolute dollar reduction, or net first-month price — to identify which headline produces the highest conversion rate.

When designing a one-time fee, select a fee name that clearly describes what you are doing for the customer, state a plausible reason, and pick a price; then decide whether to charge it, discount it, or waive it on a per-prospect basis depending on sales context.

For services requiring active customer participation (physical therapy, behavior-change programs), charge a meaningful one-time setup fee to increase customer investment and improve follow-through on the actions required to achieve results.
practices.topic: Setup Fee as Churn Reduction Mechanism

practices.text: Never reduce a stated price in real time in response to a prospect's resistance; instead, pre-structure all discounts before the conversation and present them as seller-controlled terms with a defined reason and expiry.
Consult legal counsel regarding advertising compliance requirements for discount-plus-fee structures in your specific jurisdiction before deploying this offer structure.
